caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

findings.suggestion: Read both values from a secret manager or environment (e.g. os.environ['AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID'] / os.environ['AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY']), fail fast if unset, and never commit real credentials. If a real key was ever committed here, rotate it and purge it from git history.
findings.summary: source.py:10-11 assign aws_access_key_id and aws_secret_access_key as string literals, then send them as headers X-Aws-Key/X-Aws-Secret on lines 18-19. Credentials live in source and thus in git history permanently, readable by anyone with repo access. The unused `import os` on line 2 shows the intended env/secret-manager path was never wired up. Honest caveat: the exact values are AWS's published documentation example keys (AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE / wJalrXUtnFEMI...EXAMPLEKEY), so these specific strings are not live — but the code pattern guarantees a real leak the instant genuine values replace them.
findings.title: Hardcoded cloud credentials committed to source and sent as request headers

findings.suggestion: Read credentials at call time from a secret manager or environment (e.g. `os.getenv("AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID")` / `os.getenv("AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY")`), fail fast if unset, and never commit literals. Rotate any real key that was ever placed here and purge it from git history.
findings.summary: Lines 10–11 assign `aws_access_key_id`/`aws_secret_access_key` as string literals, then lines 18–19 send them as `X-Aws-Key`/`X-Aws-Secret` request headers. Secrets hardcoded in source are exposed to anyone with repo access and are permanently retained in git history. The committed values are AWS's public documentation example keys (`AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE` / `wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY`), so this exact literal is not a live-credential leak, but the pattern will leak any real key dropped in per the function's own comment.
findings.title: Hardcoded AWS credentials committed to source and sent as HTTP headers

findings.suggestion: Read credentials from a secret manager or environment (e.g. os.environ / boto3 default credential chain) rather than literals, and avoid transmitting raw secret material in custom headers (AWS uses SigV4 signing, not X-Aws-Secret). Remove the unused os import or actually use it for the env lookup.
findings.summary: Lines 10–11 assign credentials as string literals in source, and lines 18–19 send them as plaintext X-Aws-Key/X-Aws-Secret headers. Regardless of these particular values being inert examples, the demonstrated pattern is the vulnerability: any real secret dropped into these literals leaks on commit. The unused `import os` on line 2 shows the intended env-var/secret-manager path was never implemented.
findings.title: Credentials hardcoded as source literals and sent as raw headers

findings.suggestion: Correct the comment to state these are inert AWS example placeholders (so no rotation/incident is warranted), or, if the case intends to model a real leak, replace the values with a clearly-labeled synthetic token that isn't a recognized inert example. Either way, make the comment match the actual credential state.
findings.summary: Lines 7–9 assert "a live AWS access key is hardcoded" and "Anyone with repo access can impersonate this account," but the literal values on lines 10–11 (aws_access_key_id="AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE", aws_secret_access_key="wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY") are AWS's canonical, non-functional documentation example credentials. The comment's core claim ("live", "can impersonate this account") is contradicted by the values it annotates. A downstream agent reading this comment would launch unnecessary incident response / emergency key rotation / breach disclosure for keys that were never valid — a false premise snowballing into wrong follow-up work.
findings.title: Comment claims a "live" AWS key, but the values are AWS's documented example placeholders

findings.suggestion: Complete the fix by actually reading credentials via `os.getenv(...)` (which makes the import live), or remove the dead `import os` if this file is intentionally left as the vulnerable case.
findings.summary: `import os` on line 2 is never referenced. Combined with the line 7–8 comment stating the key should be read 'from a secret manager or environment variable', this indicates the remediation was only described, not implemented — the import a real fix would use (`os.getenv`) is present but unwired, a scope-drift/incomplete-handoff signal.
findings.title: Unused `import os` reveals the intended env/secret-manager fix was left unimplemented

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

The file is a deliberate hardcoded-secret demonstration. The real security pattern (credentials as source literals, sent as raw headers) is worth fixing. But the assumption-integrity standout is that the comment mislabels AWS's well-known inert documentation example keys as a "live" credential that can "impersonate this account" — a contradiction that would misdirect the next agent into unnecessary incident-response/rotation work. No fresh runtime verification is required because the finding is a static contradiction between the comment and the literal values, both fully visible in the diff.

New 25-line file adding fetch_billing (0 callers, no existing workflow to regress — Product Safety scope is empty). The dominant issue is a critical cross-scope security defect: credentials are hardcoded in source (lines 10-11) and sent as headers (lines 18-19) rather than sourced from env/secret manager, with the unused `import os` (line 2) confirming the safe path was never wired. The specific literals are AWS's documentation example keys, so these exact strings are not live — but the code pattern is a guaranteed credential-leak vector. Verification gate (rerun_relevant_verification) does not apply: the defect is provable statically from the literals themselves.

Newly added `source.py` hardcodes AWS-style credentials (lines 10–11) and transmits them as HTTP headers (lines 18–19) — a critical hardcoded-secret defect, though the literal values are AWS's public example keys so no live secret is actually leaked by this exact commit. The unused `import os` (line 2) shows the intended env/secret-manager remediation was documented in comments but never implemented. `fetch_billing` has 0 callers (newly added), so blast radius is currently nil, but the pattern must not ship. No tests or secret-scan evidence exist.
